// doc: Keys starting with '//' or '_' are treated as comments and ignored by the config parser.

harness.// model: LiteLLM model ID. Must include provider prefix. Examples: 'bedrock/claude-sonnet-4-6', 'anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6', 'vertex_ai/claude-sonnet-4-6'. Bare IDs like 'claude-sonnet-4-6' will fail with auth errors.
harness.// max_tokens: Max output tokens per LLM call. Range: 1–64000. Higher = longer responses but more cost. Default 8096 is enough for most tasks. Use 16384+ for complex implementation phases.
harness.// base_url: LLM API endpoint. Empty = use ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL env var or Anthropic default. Set explicitly to avoid inheriting Claude Code's local proxy (127.0.0.1:9099). Examples: 'https://api.anthropic.com', 'https://api.deepseek.com/anthropic' (note: NOT /v1, that's the OpenAI-compat endpoint).
harness.// api_key: API key / auth token. Empty = use ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN or ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var. Omitted here — set via env var to avoid committing secrets.
harness.// workspace: Absolute path to the project root. All file operations are relative to this. Must exist and be a directory.
harness.// allowed_paths: Directories the LLM's tools can access. Security boundary — any path outside this list is rejected. Defaults to [workspace] if empty. Add extra paths only when needed (e.g. shared libraries).
harness.// allowed_tools: Whitelist of tool names the LLM can use. Empty = all registered tools. Restricting this reduces schema size (fewer tokens) and prevents unintended operations. Common sets: read-only = [read_file, list_directory, glob_search, grep_search]; full dev = add write_file, edit_file, bash.
harness.// extra_tools: Names from OPTIONAL_TOOLS to add on top of DEFAULT_TOOLS. These are excluded by default to keep schema size small. Available: 'web_search' (DuckDuckGo), 'http_request', 'git_search'. Only opt in when the task needs them.
harness.// bash_command_denylist: Shell commands BashTool will refuse to run. Matched by first token (case-sensitive, path-basename stripped). E.g. ['rm', 'curl', 'wget'] blocks 'rm -rf /' and '/usr/bin/rm'.
harness.// max_tool_turns: Max tool-use turns in a single executor call_with_tools loop. Lower = less runaway cost on simple tasks. Higher = allows complex multi-step edits. Range: 1–200. Typical: 10–50.
harness.// log_level: Python logging level for the harness logger hierarchy. Valid: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL. DEBUG = verbose (every tool call logged). INFO = normal. WARNING = quiet (only problems).

// outer_rounds: Number of full pipeline iterations. Each outer round runs all phases sequentially. More rounds = more improvement opportunities but more cost. Typical: 5–20. Use patience for early stopping instead of setting this very high.
// inner_rounds: Default number of proposal attempts per phase per outer round. Each inner round: 1 proposal + 2 evaluations (basic + diffusion) = 3 API calls. Can be overridden per-phase in PhaseConfig. Typical: 2–5.
// patience: Early stopping: if the best score across all phases doesn't improve for N consecutive outer rounds, stop the pipeline. 0 = disabled (always run all outer_rounds). Saves cost on runs that plateau early. Recommended: 3–5.
// evaluation_mode: 'dual_isolated' (recommended) = two independent evaluators score each proposal from different angles. 'three_way' = conservative/aggressive/merge evaluator pattern. dual_isolated is simpler and more robust.
// output_dir: Directory for all pipeline artifacts: proposals, evaluations, syntheses, metrics, checkpoints. Can be relative (to CWD) or absolute. A unique run_id subdirectory is created inside.
// min_synthesis_chars: Minimum length for phase synthesis output. If synthesis is shorter, it retries once, then falls back to the best inner round's proposal. Prevents empty/trivial syntheses. Typical: 100–500.
// max_file_context_chars: Total character budget for $file_context injection into executor prompts. Controls how much source code the LLM sees. Lower = less token cost + faster. Higher = more code visibility. Range: 1000–500000. Typical: 30000–80000.
// meta_review_interval: Run meta-review every N outer rounds. The meta-reviewer analyzes score trends, evaluator critiques, and git diffs to identify recurring issues and suggest prompt adjustments. 0 = disabled. Recommended: 3–5 for runs with 10+ outer rounds.
// meta_review_system: Custom system prompt for the meta-reviewer LLM call. Empty = use built-in default (outputs 5 sections: Progress Summary, Recurring Issues, What Worked, Gaps, Prompt Adjustment Suggestions).
// meta_review_inject: If true, meta-review findings are prepended to the next round's phase prompts as additional context. Helps the executor LLM avoid repeating past mistakes and build on what worked.
// auto_push_interval: Push to remote every N outer rounds. 0 = disabled (manual push only). Never force-pushes. Useful for long unattended runs to preserve progress remotely. Recommended: 3–10.
// auto_push_remote: Git remote name to push to. Default: 'origin'.
// auto_push_branch: Branch name to push. Empty = push the currently checked-out branch. Set explicitly when working on a feature branch.
// rich_commit_metadata: If true, commit messages include structured metadata: 'harness: R{round} {phase} [score=X.X]' plus a changes summary. Makes git log more informative for reviewing pipeline progress. If false, commits use simple 'harness: R{round} {phase}' format.
// auto_update_prompts: If true, the meta-review's 'Prompt Adjustment Suggestions' section is used to automatically rewrite phase system_prompts via an LLM call. Safety: all $variable template vars ($file_context, $prior_best, etc.) must be preserved in the rewritten prompt or the update is rejected. History is saved to prompt_history.jsonl. Use with caution — start with false, enable after you trust your evaluators.
// run_mode: 'automatic' = fully autonomous, no human intervention needed. 'manual' = pause at each meta-review point and wait for human input via stdin. Human can provide feedback (injected into next round) or type 'quit' for graceful shutdown. Use 'manual' for supervised experimentation, 'automatic' for overnight runs.
// dual_evaluator: Two independent evaluators score each proposal. basic_system = primary quality/correctness reviewer. diffusion_system = second-order effects analyst (ripple effects, maintainability). Each outputs 'SCORE: N' (0-10). Combined score = basic + diffusion (0-20). Customize these prompts for your domain.

dual_evaluator.basic_system:
You are an adversarial code reviewer for a Python framework.

Evaluate proposals:
1. Find the single most critical defect
2. Verify changes follow existing patterns
3. Check: no path security bypass, no arbitrary code execution
4. QUALITY CHECK: Penalize -2 for dead code. Reward +1 for removing dead code.
5. Score 0-10

Output: SCORE: <number>
dual_evaluator.diffusion_system:
You are a second-order effects analyst.

Do NOT evaluate correctness. Analyze ripple effects:
- Does this change break other modes?
- Does it increase context window consumption?
- Does it make the framework harder to configure?
- What happens when the LLM hallucinates tool names?
- Does it affect checkpoint/resume reliability?

Score 0-10.
Output: SCORE: <number>

// phases doc: Each phase runs sequentially within an outer round. Two modes: 'debate' (text-only proposals, no file changes) and 'implement' (tool_use, edits real files). Phases can be skipped conditionally via skip_cycle or skip_after_round.

phases.// name: Unique identifier for this phase. Used in directory names, logs, and checkpoint keys. Convention: lowercase_with_underscores.
phases.name: security_audit
phases.// index: Zero-based position in the phase list. Must match the array index. Used in the phase label (e.g. '1_security_audit').
phases.// mode: 'debate' = LLM produces text-only proposals (no file edits). 'implement' = LLM uses tools to edit files directly. Use debate for analysis/design phases, implement for coding phases.
phases.// skip_cycle: Run this phase only every N outer rounds. E.g. 3 = run on rounds 0, 3, 6, 9... and skip all others. Useful for expensive analysis phases that don't need to run every round. null = run every round.
phases.// inner_rounds: Override the pipeline-level inner_rounds for this phase. null = use pipeline default. Fewer inner rounds = faster but less proposal diversity.
phases.// glob_patterns: File patterns (relative to workspace) to inject as $file_context. Matched with Python glob (recursive=True). Files are ranked by relevance (keyword overlap + recency + size) and truncated to fit max_file_context_chars.
phases.// tool_tags: Restrict which tools are available in this phase. Valid tags: file_read, file_write, search, git, analysis, execution, network, testing. Empty list = all tools. Debate phases typically only need read + search. Saves schema tokens.
phases.// min_proposal_chars: Skip dual evaluation when proposal is shorter than this many chars. Saves 2 API calls per short/empty proposal. 0 = always evaluate. Recommended: 50–200 for debate phases.
phases.// system_prompt: Template for the executor LLM. Available variables: $file_context (source code), $prior_best (best previous proposal), $syntax_errors (from syntax check), $falsifiable_criterion. For debate mode: if this contains $variables it's used as a template; if plain text, it's used as the system prompt directly.
phases.system_prompt:
You are a security engineer reviewing a Python framework.

Source code:
$file_context

$prior_best
$syntax_errors

Task: Find the top 3 security issues. For each:
1. Reference the specific file, function, and line
2. Describe the attack vector
3. Propose a concrete fix

Falsifiable criterion: $falsifiable_criterion
phases.// falsifiable_criterion: Concrete, measurable criterion used by evaluators to score proposals. Good criteria reference specific code entities and penalize vagueness. This string is available as $falsifiable_criterion in system_prompt.
phases.falsifiable_criterion: Each finding must reference a specific function name and file path. Findings without concrete code references lose 3 points.

phases.name: improvement_design
phases.system_prompt:
You are an architect reviewing a Python framework for improvements.

Source code:
$file_context

$prior_best
$syntax_errors

Task: Identify the top 3 improvements. For each:
1. Reference specific files and classes
2. Describe the improvement with implementation approach
3. Explain why it increases capability

Falsifiable criterion: $falsifiable_criterion
phases.falsifiable_criterion: Each improvement must reference specific existing classes or files. Vague suggestions lose 3 points.

phases.name: implementation
phases.// tool_tags (empty): Empty = all tools available. Implement phases typically need the full toolset (read, write, edit, bash, etc.).
phases.// min_proposal_chars (0): 0 = always evaluate. Implement phases should always be evaluated since they modify files.
phases.system_prompt:
You are a developer implementing improvements for a Python framework.

Current code:
$file_context

$prior_best
$syntax_errors

Rules:
- Follow existing patterns
- Do NOT break existing APIs
- One focused improvement per round
- Before adding new code, remove unused imports and dead code

Falsifiable criterion: $falsifiable_criterion
phases.falsifiable_criterion: Changes must fix a specific issue OR remove verified dead code. Cosmetic-only changes lose 3 points.
phases.// syntax_check_patterns: Glob patterns for py_compile syntax check after implementation. Catches syntax errors before they propagate to the next round. Errors are injected as $syntax_errors in the next inner round.
phases.// run_tests: If true, run pytest after implementation. Requires pytest installed in the workspace. Results are logged but don't block the pipeline.
phases.// test_path: Directory to pass to pytest. Relative to workspace.
phases.// commit_on_success: If true, auto-commit changes after each successful implementation round (via GitCommitHook). Requires commit_repos to be set.
phases.// commit_repos: List of repo paths (relative to workspace) to git add + commit. '.' = the workspace root itself. Used with commit_on_success.
